verdict_reasoning: ATG9A is a multi-pass transmembrane protein (4 TM helices, homotrimeric) that predominantly resides intracellularly—cycling between the trans-Golgi network, endosomes, and phagophore assembly sites under basal conditions. However, there is robust, gene-specific evidence of a genuine plasma membrane (PM) pool: (1) super-resolution TIRF microscopy directly images FLAG-ATG9A at the PM in HeLa cells; (2) surface biotinylation assays biochemically confirm ATG9A at the cell surface; (3) APEX2-ATG9A EM shows DAB deposits at the PM. Critically, this PM pool increases substantially upon PM damage (gasdermin, MLKL, digitonin) or EGF stimulation, indicating the dominant surface-accessible fraction is condition-induced. Under basal conditions only a minority fraction traffics through the PM as part of its endocytic recycling itinerary (plasma membrane → early endosome → recycling endosome → TGN). The predominant compartment is intracellular (TGN/endosomes), making a 'yes' verdict inappropriate. The best fit is 'dual_localization': documented PM pool alongside a dominant endomembrane compartment, with active cycling. No therapeutic antibody/ADC program targeting the extracellular face is on record, and no GPI anchor or stable ectodomain is present—all loops are small given the 4-TM architecture with no large extracellular domain.
predicted_key_uncertainty: The extent and orientation of extracellular loops in the 4-TM topology is not fully defined; if loops are entirely intraluminal/cytoplasmic, a binder from the extracellular face may not engage the protein body even when it is at the PM.
